study.journal: Combes A, Hajage D, Capellier G, et al. Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation for Severe Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome. N Engl J Med. 2018;378(21):1965-1975.

population_context.population_description: Patients with very severe ARDS defined by one of three criteria: PaO2:FiO2 ratio <50 mm Hg for >3 hours, PaO2:FiO2 <80 mm Hg for >6 hours, or arterial blood pH <7.25 with PaCO2 ≥60 mm Hg for >6 hours, who had undergone endotracheal intubation and been receiving ventilation for less than 7 days.
population_context.risk_group: Patients with severe ARDS refractory to conventional mechanical ventilation despite optimization
population_context.exclusions: Age <18 years; mechanical ventilation ≥7 days; pregnancy; weight >1 kg/cm or BMI >45; chronic respiratory insufficiency on oxygen or noninvasive ventilation; cardiac failure requiring venoarterial ECMO; heparin-induced thrombocytopenia history; cancer with life expectancy <5 years; moribund condition or SAPS-II >90; non-drug-induced coma after cardiac arrest; irreversible neurologic injury; decision to withhold/withdraw life-sustaining therapies; expected difficulty obtaining vascular access for ECMO; ECMO device unavailable.

intervention.name: Early venovenous ECMO
intervention.parameter_values: Anticoagulation target: activated partial-thromboplastin time 40-55 seconds or anti-Xa activity 0.2-0.3 IU/mL
intervention.timing: Immediate initiation at randomization in ECMO group; within 2 hours for non-ECMO center patients

comparator.description: Continued conventional mechanical ventilation treatment according to the increased recruitment strategy from the Express trial, with strong encouragement of neuromuscular blocking agents and prolonged prone positioning. Recruitment maneuvers, inhaled nitric oxide, inhaled prostacyclin, or intravenous almitrine allowed at discretion. Crossover to ECMO permitted for refractory hypoxemia (SpO2 <80% for >6 hours despite available therapies).

strengths: Prospective enrollment of patients with very severe, well-defined ARDS
Independent data and safety monitoring committee oversight
Standardized protocols for both ECMO and conventional treatment arms
Stratified randomization by center and ventilation duration
limitations: Trial stopped early per sequential design, potentially limiting power to detect differences
28% crossover rate in control group may have affected treatment effect estimates
Heterogeneous international settings may introduce practice variation
Relatively small sample size achieved (249 vs. planned 331)
adverse_events: Bleeding events leading to transfusion more common in ECMO group (46% vs. 28%; ARD 18 percentage points; 95% CI 6-30)
Severe thrombocytopenia more common in ECMO group (27% vs. 16%; ARD 11 percentage points; 95% CI 0-21)
Ischemic stroke less common in ECMO group (0% vs. 5%; ARD -5 percentage points; 95% CI -10 to -2)
High mortality in crossover patients (57% of 35 control patients who crossed over died)

applicability.generalizability_notes: Findings apply to patients with very severe ARDS meeting specific PaO2:FiO2 and pH/PaCO2 criteria who have been mechanically ventilated <7 days. Results suggest early ECMO does not significantly reduce mortality compared to conventional treatment with ECMO available as rescue therapy. Increased bleeding and thrombocytopenia risks with ECMO should inform decision-making. Applicability may be limited to centers with ECMO expertise and infrastructure.

quoted_sentences.design: In an international clinical trial, we randomly assigned patients with very severe ARDS, as indicated by one of three criteria — a ratio of partial pressure of arterial oxygen (Pao2) to the fraction of inspired oxygen (Fio2) of less than 50 mm Hg for more than 3 hours; a Pao2:Fio2 of less than 80 mm Hg for more than 6 hours; or an arterial blood pH of less than 7.25 with a partial pressure of arterial carbon dioxide of at least 60 mm Hg for more than 6 hours — to receive immediate venovenous ECMO (ECMO group) or continued conventional treatment (control group).
quoted_sentences.intervention: Patients assigned to the ECMO group underwent percutaneous venovenous cannulation. Anticoagulation was achieved with unfractionated heparin that was adjusted to a target activated partial-thromboplastin time of 40 to 55 seconds or anti-Xa activity between 0.2 and 0.3 IU per milliliter.
quoted_sentences.results: At 60 days, 44 of 124 patients (35%) in the ECMO group and 57 of 125 (46%) in the control group had died (relative risk, 0.76; 95% confidence interval [CI], 0.55 to 1.04; P = 0.09).
quoted_sentences.outcome: Among patients with very severe ARDS, 60-day mortality was not significantly lower with ECMO than with a strategy of conventional mechanical ventilation that included ECMO as rescue therapy.
